title: Recession unlikely, but war fallout yet to be felt: ANZ
full_text: Other Editions 04 May 2026 Business Recession unlikely, but war fallout yet to be felt: ANZ The Australian Max Aitchison 2 May 2026 725 words English AUSTLN 26 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. ANZ chief Nuno Matos has warned the most damaging fallout from the Iran war is still to be felt and, while some borrowers will hit the wall, Australia’s economy will not slide into recession. The bank reported a 6 per cent lift in first-half cash profit to $3.78bn. Mr Matos, who will mark one year in the CEO role on May 12, told investors that, as Australia’s most international bank, ANZ had a “front-row seat to global developments”. “Much of the potential impact of this crisis remains ahead of us, but the longer the flow of oil is constrained, the greater the chance the crisis shifts from being primarily an inflation challenge, to much more a supply and growth challenge,” he said, adding that consumer and business confidence in Australia was ­“materially weaker”. Despite this, Mr Matos insisted that both his corporate and ­retail customers were well prepared. He said he had not witnessed any material change in borrower behaviour or retail customers entering hardship or receiving assistance. “Spending and business conditions have only been impacted modestly so far, and employment growth is stable,” he said. “This supports our central expectation that Australia will avoid a recession, although the situation is extremely dynamic, and we are prepared for a range of outcomes.” The bank increased its bad loan provisions by $175m to account for the “potential impacts of the Middle East conflict”. This comes after both National Australia Bank and Westpac increased their buffers for bad debts in response to the conflict. ANZ’s $3.78bn cash profit for the six months to March 31, which is up 6 per cent on the first half of the 2025 financial year, beat market expectations. Statutory profit was up 9 per cent to $3.65bn, while return on equity was up 9.1 per cent compared to the second half of FY25. However, lending fell 1 per cent to $822bn, while deposits only grew by a meagre 3 per cent to $771bn. The bank declared an 83c-a-share final dividend, partially franked, matching consensus. Mr Matos said the result was a vindication of his radical transformation of the bank, which has included the sacking of 4500 people and a major project designed to streamline its offering and bring forward the integration of Suncorp Bank. Chief financial officer Farhan Faruqi told investors that ANZ had made 78 per cent of its planned redundancies, with a further 770 to leave by September. However, the result was met with market trepidation. ANZ shares closed at $35.61, down 2.84 per cent. Citi analyst Thomas Strong said the bank’s revenue of $11.2bn missed expectations. “ANZ’s capital continues to look strong, as do its provisions which reflect its different book mix versus peers,” Mr Strong told his clients. “Overall, we think the result will have a mixed reception given the revenue miss, but we believe on balance the thesis for the stock remains unchanged.” Jarden analyst Matt Wilson said investors needed to see more evidence of the progress of Suncorp Bank’s integration, the delivery of ANZ Plus into one digital front-end, and the lender’s wider progress on non-financial risk ­remediation. “ANZ aspires to extend and leverage leadership in institutional and NZ banking while closing gaps in Australian retail and business. We need clarity on approach here,” Mr Wilson told clients. Mr Matos said his retail customers had entered the ongoing oil crisis with cash to spare. “However, in recent weeks, consumers have needed to sharply increase spending on transport, leaving them with less discretionary spending,” he said. “We will continue to monitor emerging pressures and support our customers with appropriate assistance.” ANZ’s cost-to-income ratio, which is a key measure of a bank’s efficiency, improved significantly, from 65.5 per cent in the second half of financial year 2025 to 49.4 per cent. This marks the first time the key metric has dipped below 50 per cent in several years. Market leader Commonwealth Bank has a cost-to-income ratio in the mid-40s. Mr Matos’s tenure has been marked by radical cost-cutting, mass lay-offs and an overhaul of the bank’s culture. The Australian-20260502-Australian-26-000500605366 Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: ANZ's CEO Nuno Matos has indicated that while the fallout from the Iran war is yet to be fully realised, Australia is unlikely to enter a recession. The bank reported a 6% increase in first-half cash profit, but has increased its bad loan provisions due to potential impacts from the conflict. Despite a modest decline in lending, ANZ's cost-to-income ratio improved significantly, reflecting ongoing cost-cutting measures.
publication:
Chalmers talks a big game - it's time to act on intergenerational fairness
The Age02 May 20261,330 wordsMillie Muroi

When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers "entitled" for wanting more.
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: ANZ's cash profit rose by 6% to $3.78 billion for the first half of the financial year.
The bank has increased bad loan provisions by $175 million in response to the Middle East conflict.
ANZ's cost-to-income ratio improved to 49.4%, marking a significant efficiency gain.
relevance: Understanding ANZ's financial performance and economic outlook is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals as it impacts risk assessment and investment strategies.
